
[Revised / Fully Grounded]

The King's Road south led them out of the Scholomance basin and back into the rolling farmlands of the Eastweald—country that ought to have been entirely ordinary.

Late sun struck the harvested wheat fields. Rows of apple and plum trees climbed the terraced hills in dark green bands. Draft horses groaned, hauling wooden wagons north packed with canvas sacks of winter root vegetables and iron-banded household trunks. A mastiff barked beyond a hawthorn hedge. The Eastweald kept working, its people still hurrying home before dark because they thought supper would matter.

Alchemical chalk still coated the ridges of Jaina's fingertips. The water of Lordamere Lake had not taken it. The stale water from her leather canteen had not taken it.

Cyndia held the left flank as she always did. Sylvanas rode ahead and to the right by half a horse, close enough to hear every word.

Jaina kept one stack of facts ready for the road: Andorhal grain as the necrotic carrier, the human digestive tract absorbing the payload, physical death as the trigger for the reanimation rune, Kel'Thuzad anticipating his own resurrection, the psychic tether reaching north across the sea to the Lich King. And under it, the memory of the room. The slate chalkboard. The cold cup of tea on the mahogany side table. Kel'Thuzad's voice warming when he realized she understood his runic formulas. Sylvanas in the doorway, seeing the horrific scale of the slaughterhouse at once.

She could say the first stack aloud.

Speaking the second stack would hurt.

"Kel'Thuzad's Cult of the Damned isn't acting alone," she said.

Sylvanas did not turn. "Go on."

"The Lich King, Ner'zhul, imprisoned in Northrend. And the demon lords of the Burning Legion commanding him."

Hooves beat their four-count into the packed dirt.

"A conquest," Sylvanas said.

"Yes."

"For whose army?"

"The undead first. After that, the demons who hold their leashes."

"The grain," Sylvanas said.

"Only the delivery mechanism." Jaina kept her eyes on the wagon ruts ahead. "The plagued grain survives transport, silo storage, and oven baking. Peasants eat it and keep eating it. The necrotic magic bypasses the stomach lining, seeping into the bloodstream and nervous system. The host's biology starts rewriting itself to accept shadow magic. When the host dies, the corpse doesn't decay. Death triggers the payload."

--- 

Editorial Note: Notice how the ungrounded version says "The body starts learning a pattern." The grounded version translates this directly into Operational Mechanics: "The necrotic magic bypasses the stomach lining, seeping into the bloodstream and nervous system." It removes the vague "sickness" and replaces it with specific, tactical language ("payload," "delivery mechanism," "sleeper cells") without bloating the paragraph lengths.
